metadata.notes: Pre-computed barge route/cost combinations for bulk cement movement on the US inland waterway system. Costs reflect typical contract rates including fuel surcharges. Downstream transit times assume loaded barges; upstream times assume empties returning.
sources: US Army Corps Lock Performance Monitoring System

scenarios.origin: Houston, TX (Houston Ship Channel / Turning Basin)
scenarios.destination: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: GIWW width restrictions limit tow to 2-wide x 3-long configuration
scenarios.notes: ICW route is sheltered from open Gulf weather. Cement loaded at import terminals on Houston Ship Channel. Congestion at Harvey Lock (mile 6.4 GIWW West) can add 6-12 hours.

scenarios.origin: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: Open river allows full 5-wide x 3-long tow configuration below Cairo
scenarios.notes: All upstream movement on Lower Miss. No locks. Current averages 3-4 mph which impacts upstream transit. Memphis terminals serve tri-state cement market (TN, AR, MS).

scenarios.route_name: New Orleans to St. Louis (Lower + Middle Mississippi)
scenarios.origin: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
scenarios.destination: St. Louis, MO (Mississippi River Mile 179-185 Upper)
scenarios.waterway: Lower Mississippi River + Middle Mississippi River
lock_names: Lock and Dam 27 (Chain of Rocks, Mile 185.5)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: 15-barge tow to Cairo; may break to smaller tows for lock passage above confluence. Chain of Rocks Lock limits tow to 15 barges (1200 ft chamber).
scenarios.notes: No locks from NOLA to Cairo IL (mile 953). Two locks above Cairo on Middle Miss to reach St. Louis. Ste. Genevieve MO cement plants (Holcim, Buzzi) are major origins for return loads. Winter ice can delay traffic Dec-Feb.

scenarios.route_name: New Orleans to Cincinnati (via Ohio River)
scenarios.origin: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
lock_names: Lock and Dam 52 (being replaced by Olmsted)
John T. Myers Lock (Ohio River Mile 846)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: 15-barge tow on Lower Miss; reduced to smaller cuts for Ohio River locks (most have 1200 ft chambers, some 600 ft requiring double-locking).
scenarios.notes: Ohio River is heavily locked. Double-locking at 600-ft chambers adds significant transit time. Cincinnati area served by multiple river terminals. Essroc/Heidelberg and Buzzi plants in region provide competitive rail alternatives.

scenarios.route_name: New Orleans to Chicago (via Illinois Waterway)
scenarios.origin: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
scenarios.waterway: Lower Mississippi River + Illinois Waterway (Illinois River + Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: 15-barge tow on Lower Miss and lower Illinois River; reduced to 6 or 9 barges for upper Illinois Waterway locks (600 ft chambers).
scenarios.notes: Illinois Waterway is a critical corridor for Chicago-area cement supply. 8 locks total on Illinois Waterway. Brandon Road Lock undergoing Asian carp barrier construction (potential delays). Chicago terminals at Cal-Sag Channel and Calumet Harbor serve large metro market.

scenarios.origin: Houston, TX (Houston Ship Channel / Turning Basin)
scenarios.waterway: Gulf Intracoastal Waterway + Lower Mississippi River
scenarios.tow_config_notes: 6-barge tow through ICW; may combine into larger tow at NOLA for Mississippi River transit. Tow reconfiguration at Port Allen or NOLA anchorage.
scenarios.notes: Combined ICW + Lower Miss route for imported cement discharged at Houston terminals destined for Memphis/Mid-South markets. 6-barge ICW tow limits per-shipment volume. Often more economical to transload to 15-barge tow at NOLA.

scenarios.tow_config_notes: Full 15-barge tow on open Lower Mississippi River
scenarios.notes: Baton Rouge has multiple deep-water terminals handling cement imports. Shorter transit to Memphis than NOLA origin. Giant Cement (Heidelberg) Baton Rouge grinding station is major origin.

scenarios.origin: Cairo, IL (Mississippi River Mile 953 / Ohio River confluence)
scenarios.destination: St. Louis, MO (Mississippi River Mile 179-185 Upper)
lock_names: Lock and Dam 27 (Chain of Rocks, Mile 185.5)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: 15-barge tow feasible; Chain of Rocks Lock has 1200 ft chamber accommodating full tow
scenarios.notes: Short segment connecting Ohio River traffic to St. Louis market. Cairo is transshipment point where Ohio River tows join Mississippi River. Two locks required to reach St. Louis harbor. Ste. Genevieve cement plants (Holcim, Buzzi Unicem) are at river mile ~120 Upper Miss, between Cairo and St. Louis.

scenarios.origin: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
scenarios.tow_config_notes: Smaller 6-barge tow for partial shipments or fleet repositioning
scenarios.notes: 6-barge configuration used when full 15-barge tow cannot be assembled or when smaller parcels are needed. Higher per-ton cost due to fixed crew/fuel spread over fewer tons. Often combined with other commodities in mixed tow.

scenarios.route_name: New Orleans to Ste. Genevieve (Lower + Middle Mississippi)
scenarios.origin: New Orleans, LA (Lower Mississippi River Mile 95-105)
scenarios.destination: Ste. Genevieve, MO (Upper Mississippi River Mile 120)
scenarios.waterway: Lower Mississippi River + Middle Mississippi River
scenarios.tow_config_notes: No locks required; Ste. Genevieve is below Chain of Rocks Lock on the open river
scenarios.notes: Ste. Genevieve is a major cement production hub (Holcim Ste. Genevieve plant is one of the largest in the US at 4.6M tons/year). Import clinker or supplementary materials moved upriver. No lock passage required as Ste. Genevieve is on open Middle Mississippi below Chain of Rocks.
